title: Wife's Rights to Mahr (Dower) in Bangladesh: What the Law Says
metaTitle: Mahr (Dower) Rights of Wife in Bangladesh – Complete Legal Guide
metaDescription: What is mahr (dower) in Bangladesh? When is it payable, how much can a wife claim, and what happens if the husband refuses? Expert family lawyer guide.
heroIntro: Mahr (also spelled mehr or dower) is a mandatory gift from husband to wife in Islamic marriage law and is a legally enforceable right in Bangladesh. Understanding when it is due, how much it should be, and how to claim it is essential for any Muslim woman.
toc: Types of Mahr: Prompt and Deferred
How the Mahr Amount is Determined

sections.content:
Mahr (or dower) is a mandatory payment or gift given by a husband to his wife under Muslim personal law, as recorded in the Kabinnama (marriage registration certificate). Under the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 and the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939, mahr is a legal right — not a gift that the husband can choose to give or withhold. The wife can sue to recover it in the Family Court.

If the Kabinnama does not specify a mahr amount, "proper dower" (mahr-e-misl) applies — determined by customary standards based on the wife's family status, father's social position, and locality.

sections.h2: Types of Mahr: Prompt and Deferred
sections.content:
Mahr is typically divided into two parts in the Kabinnama:

  • Prompt Mahr (Mahr-e-Mu'ajjal): Payable immediately on marriage, on demand, or at a specified time. If not paid, the wife can refuse to cohabit without forfeiting her rights.
  • Deferred Mahr (Mahr-e-Mu'ajjal): Payable on dissolution of marriage (by divorce or death of husband). If the husband dies without paying, the deferred mahr is a debt of the estate — payable before inheritance distribution.

Mahr becomes immediately enforceable:

  • On husband's demand from wife for cohabitation (prompt mahr must be paid on demand)
  • On divorce — both prompt and deferred mahr become immediately due unless waived by khula
  • On death of the husband — deferred mahr is treated as a debt of the estate

A wife is entitled to refuse conjugal rights until prompt mahr is paid, without it affecting her own maintenance rights.

An unpaid mahr can be sued for in the Family Court under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985. File a plaint for recovery of mahr, attaching a certified copy of the Kabinnama. The court will examine the Kabinnama, determine the amount due, and pass a money decree against the husband. The decree can be enforced through attachment of his salary, bank account, or property.

Contact a family lawyer in Uttara for help filing a mahr recovery case.

On divorce initiated by the husband (talaq), the wife retains the full right to both prompt and deferred mahr. She cannot be forced to waive it as a condition of divorce.

However, in khula (wife-initiated divorce), the wife typically waives the deferred mahr or returns the prompt mahr as consideration for the husband's agreement. Courts will not force the husband into khula — his agreement is needed unless the wife seeks judicial khula through Family Court on valid grounds.

Advocate Md. Shah Alam in Uttara handles all mahr and family law disputes.

faqs.question: Can a wife be forced to give up her mahr?
faqs.answer: No. Mahr is an absolute right. A husband cannot legally compel a wife to waive it as a condition of divorce. Any such agreement signed under duress is voidable.

faqs.question: What if the mahr amount in the Kabinnama is very low?
faqs.answer: "Proper dower" (mahr-e-misl) provides a minimum benchmark. If the specified amount is unconscionably low, a court may still apply mahr-e-misl standards in certain circumstances — consult a lawyer.

faqs.question: Is mahr taxable in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Generally no. Mahr is treated as a personal right/gift under family law, not as income. However, large mahr transactions should be documented carefully.

faqs.question: Can mahr be claimed after the death of the husband?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deferred mahr is a debt of the estate and must be paid from the deceased husband's assets before the remaining estate is distributed among heirs.
